kp.low: Low activity — auroras only near poles
kp.g1: G1 Minor storm — auroras at high latitudes
kp.g2: G2 Moderate — possible power grid fluctuations
kp.g4: G4 Severe — widespread grid and HF radio impact
kp.g5: G5 Extreme — major disruptions, auroras at low latitudes

xray.a: Minimal flare activity — no impact expected
xray.b: Very weak flare — no disruption expected
xray.c: Weak flare — minor radio interference possible
xray.m: Moderate flare — possible HF radio blackout
xray.x: Major flare — radio blackout and storm risk

storm.unsettled: Minor disturbances, mainly at polar regions
storm.g2: Long-duration power systems affected, aurora above 55°
storm.g3: Voltage corrections needed, GPS degradation possible
storm.g4: Widespread power control issues, satellite errors

products.heroTitle: Tools built for space weather intelligence.
products.heroSub: Products for monitoring, predicting, and integrating space weather. From live dashboards to developer APIs and automated alerts.
products.d1Desc: Monitor space weather in real time. Track geomagnetic activity, solar wind, X-ray flux, near-Earth objects, ISS position, aurora forecasts, and ML-powered storm predictions — all from a single interface, updated continuously from NOAA, NASA, and global observatories.
products.d2Desc: Integrate real-time space weather data into your applications. Low-latency REST endpoints for Kp index, solar wind, X-ray flux, and anomaly alerts — plus a predictive layer with 3-hour ML forecasts and confidence intervals.
products.d3Desc: Get alerted the moment space weather conditions change. Know when storms intensify, custom thresholds are exceeded, or satellite conditions shift — delivered via webhook or email the instant it happens.

about.heroTitle: Built for those who need to know what's happening in space before it affects Earth.
about.missionP1: Space weather is not an abstract concern. Geomagnetic storms disrupt power grids, degrade GPS accuracy, and saturate satellite communications — sometimes with less than an hour's warning. Solar flares can knock out HF radio across entire hemispheres. A well-timed coronal mass ejection can bring a continent's infrastructure to a halt.
about.missionP2: The data to see all of this coming has always existed. NOAA's Space Weather Prediction Center, NASA's real-time feeds, the Kyoto World Data Center — they publish continuously. The problem is that the data is scattered, formatted for researchers, and arrives too raw for anyone who just needs a clear answer right now.
about.missionP3: Astraeusio is the layer between that raw data and the people who need to act on it: satellite operators, grid engineers, amateur radio operators, aviation teams, researchers, and anyone who takes infrastructure seriously. We ingest everything in real time, run it through an LSTM model trained on two decades of NOAA history, and surface what matters.
about.built1Desc: Most readings refresh every 60 seconds. Each source runs independently — a delay in one feed never affects the others.
about.built2Desc: Every forecast includes a lower and upper bound, so you can see not just what's predicted but how certain that prediction is.
about.built3Desc: All data is stored and served locally. The dashboard loads in milliseconds regardless of how many people are using it.
about.built4Desc: If the ML model is unavailable, the last known forecast is shown with a clear timestamp — never a silent blank.
about.src1Desc: Real-time Kp index, solar wind (DSCOVR satellite), X-ray flux (GOES), IMF Bz, Dst index, and space weather alerts. SWPC is the authoritative operational source for space weather in the US — when a G3 storm is in progress, this is where that classification comes from.
about.src2Desc: Near-Earth Object feed (NeoWs), Astronomy Picture of the Day, and EPIC Earth imagery from the DSCOVR spacecraft. NASA's open API program makes this data available without rate restrictions for reasonable use.
about.src3Desc: Two-line element sets for the entire Starlink constellation, updated on each poll cycle. TLEs are the standard format for satellite tracking — Celestrak maintains one of the most reliable public mirrors.
about.src4Desc: Dst (Disturbance Storm Time) index, a measure of the ring current intensity around Earth during geomagnetic storms. Kyoto WDC is the global reference for Dst — the index is computed there and distributed through NOAA.
about.contactDesc: For questions about the API, data accuracy, licensing, or anything else — email is the fastest path.

docs.authIntro: All API endpoints (except public ones) require authentication. Astraeusio supports two authentication methods.
docs.authJwtDesc: Obtained via POST /auth/login. Valid for 24 hours. Use for dashboard access and personal scripts. JWT requests do not consume your API quota.
docs.authApiKeyDesc: Generated from the API Keys page in your dashboard. Prefixed with ast_. API key requests count against your plan quota. Suitable for server-to-server integrations.
docs.authPublicNote: Public endpoints (/api/public/*) require no authentication and are not rate-limited — they return single latest readings for live widgets.
docs.baseUrlIntro: All API requests are made to:
docs.baseUrlDevNote: During local development the server runs on http://localhost:3000.
docs.quickStartIntro: Fetch the latest Kp index using curl with a JWT token:
docs.apiRefIntro: All data endpoints return JSON. Timestamps in time_tag fields are ISO-8601 UTC strings. Numeric values use scaled integers to avoid floating-point storage — see field descriptions below.
docs.epKp: Returns the last 24 hours of 1-minute Kp index readings from NOAA SWPC. kp_index is the integer class; estimated_kp is the precise value (÷100 = Kp).
docs.epKp3h: Official NOAA 3-hour Kp values for the last 7 days. These are the definitive planetary Kp values, revised as new data arrives.
docs.epSolarWind: 1-minute real-time solar wind measurements from the NOAA DSCOVR/ACE spacecraft at L1. Speed in km/s, density in p/cm³, temperature in K.
docs.epXray: GOES X-ray flux from the primary satellite, 2-minute cadence. Two energy channels: 0.1–0.8 nm (long) and 0.05–0.4 nm (short). Flux in W/m².
docs.epImf: Interplanetary Magnetic Field measurements from DSCOVR. Bz is the north-south component (negative = southward = geoeffective). Values in nT.
docs.epDst: Dst (Disturbance Storm Time) index from the Kyoto World Data Center via NOAA. Measures ring current strength. Values in nT; negative = geomagnetic storm.
docs.epAlerts: NOAA SWPC space weather alerts, watches, and warnings. Returns all active products from the last 7 days.
docs.epForecast: ML-generated Kp forecast for the next 3 hours. Uses an LSTM neural network with Monte Carlo Dropout for uncertainty estimation (50 inference passes). Returns predicted Kp with 95% confidence interval.
docs.epIss: Current ISS position from Open Notify. Updated every 5 seconds. Altitude in km, velocity in km/h.
docs.epApod: NASA Astronomy Picture of the Day. Returns the current day's image or video with title and explanation.
docs.epNeo: Near-Earth objects from NASA NeoWs for the current 7-day window. Includes close approach distance (in lunar distances and km), diameter, velocity, and hazard status.
docs.epEpic: Latest NASA EPIC (Earth Polychromatic Imaging Camera) images from DSCOVR. Returns up to 20 most recent frames with centroid coordinates.
docs.epExoplanets: Top 100 confirmed exoplanets from the NASA Exoplanet Archive, ordered by shortest orbital period. Includes host star, orbital period, radius, and mass.
docs.epStarlink: Full Starlink constellation TLE data from Celestrak. Returns NORAD catalog ID, name, and two TLE lines for orbital propagation. Updated hourly.
docs.epAnomalies: Locally detected space weather anomalies. Five checks run every 60 seconds: Kp storm (≥G1/G4), solar wind speed (>700/>900 km/s), X-ray flare (M/X class), asteroid close approach (<1 LD), and ML storm forecast.
docs.epSummary: Aggregated space weather statistics for the selected time range. Includes average and peak Kp, peak solar wind, X-ray class summary, anomaly count, and asteroid approaches.
docs.epExport: CSV export of raw Kp, solar wind, and X-ray readings for the selected time range. Returns Content-Type: text/csv with a filename attachment header.
docs.rateLimitsIntro: Rate limits apply only to requests authenticated with an API key (ast_ prefix). JWT dashboard requests are unmetered and do not consume quota.
docs.rateLimitsWhatDesc: Every successful API call authenticated with an ast_ API key increments your counter. Requests that return 401 or 403 before reaching the route handler do not count. The /api/public/* endpoints are always free and unmetered.
docs.rateLimitsHeaderDesc: When a limit is exceeded the server returns 429 Too Many Requests:
docs.rateLimitsMonitorNote: Monitor your usage at any time:
docs.webhooksIntro: Webhooks let you receive real-time HTTP POST notifications when space weather anomalies are detected. Requires a Pro plan or higher.
docs.webhookSetupDesc: Register a webhook endpoint from the API Keys page in your dashboard, or via the API:
docs.webhookSecretNote: Response includes your signing secret — save it immediately, it is shown only once:
docs.webhookPayloadNote: Astraeusio sends a JSON POST to your endpoint with the following structure:
docs.webhookHmacDesc: Every delivery is signed with HMAC-SHA256 using your webhook secret. Always verify the signature before processing the payload.
docs.webhookTimeoutNote: Webhook deliveries use a 5-second timeout. Failed deliveries are not retried automatically. You can re-register the endpoint if needed.
docs.emailAlertsIntro: Email alerts send you a notification when live space weather readings exceed your personal thresholds. Requires a Developer plan or higher. Alerts are throttled to at most one email per hour regardless of how many thresholds are exceeded simultaneously.
docs.emailAlertsDashDesc: Go to API Keys → Email Alerts in your dashboard. Enable the toggle, set your Kp and solar wind thresholds, then click Save.
docs.emailAlertsFormatDesc: Alert emails are sent from the configured SMTP_FROM address with subject Astraeusio Space Weather Alert and list which thresholds were exceeded along with the measured values.
docs.errorCodesIntro: All error responses have a consistent JSON body:

landing.heroTitle: See space weather before it impacts Earth.
landing.heroSub: Real-time monitoring of solar activity and geomagnetic storms — before they disrupt satellites, GPS, and communications.
landing.heroStat: 3-hour advance warnings for space weather events affecting Earth.
landing.c1Body: Track geomagnetic activity in real time and understand when conditions are likely to intensify — before they disrupt power grids, communications, or satellite operations.
landing.c2Body: See when and where auroras may appear based on current space weather conditions — down to your latitude.
landing.c3Body: Follow ISS and Starlink constellations with continuously updated orbital data — and anticipate disruptions from geomagnetic storms.
landing.c4Body: Identify sudden changes in solar wind, radiation, and geomagnetic indices — before they impact GPS accuracy, HF radio, or power infrastructure.
landing.liveDesc: Real-time data streams updated continuously from global observatories and space agencies.
landing.p1Body: Anticipate near-term space weather activity using machine learning models trained on decades of historical data.
landing.p2Body: Forecasts include probability ranges to reflect uncertainty in solar activity dynamics.
landing.p3Body: Identify emerging geomagnetic disturbances before they fully develop.
landing.howBody: We combine real-time observational data with predictive modeling from global space weather agencies and observatories.
landing.howMl: A machine learning forecasting system processes time-series space weather data to estimate short-term geomagnetic activity levels.
landing.techNote: API access available for integration into research, monitoring systems, and automation workflows.
landing.sourcesNote: Trusted space and atmospheric data providers
landing.s2: NASA NeoWs, EPIC, and APOD datasets
landing.a1desc: Track aurora visibility and solar storms from your location in real time.
landing.a2desc: Access time-series space weather data and ML forecast outputs for analysis and modeling.
landing.a3desc: Get advance warnings before geomagnetic events affect orbital operations and communications.
landing.a4desc: Integrate live space weather data, anomaly alerts, and Kp forecasts into your apps via REST API.
landing.a5desc: Monitor conditions that affect GPS accuracy, HF radio communications, and power grid stability.
landing.ctaSub: Get real-time insights and predictive intelligence for space weather activity affecting Earth.
landing.ctaBody: Create a free account and start tracking now.
landing.footerNote: Astraeusio · Built on open scientific data · Powered by NOAA, NASA, and open-access space weather APIs · BSL 1.1
landing.techModelVal: LSTM neural network with MC Dropout
landing.techTrainingVal: 20+ years of NOAA Kp historical data
landing.techDetectionVal: Multi-signal anomaly pipeline (Kp, solar wind, X-ray, NEO)
landing.ctaSub: Space weather is live and constantly changing. Sign up free — see current conditions the moment you log in.
